summary: The 24th and final Tirthankara of Jainism's current cosmic half-cycle, traditionally dated to the 6th century BCE and regarded as the founder of Jainism as practiced today. Born Vardhamana to King Siddhartha and Queen Trishala after 14 or 16 auspicious dreams foretold his birth, he renounced society, practiced extreme asceticism (including total nakedness), and attained Kevala Jnana (omniscience) after years of meditation. Successor to the 23rd tirthankara Parshvanatha, he added a fifth Great Vow to Parshvanatha's four and taught ahimsa (nonviolence) and aparigraha (non-possession) as central doctrines. His oral teachings were compiled by his chief disciple Gautama Swami (Indrabhuti Gautama) into the Agamas.

works.name: Mahavira
works.description: The 24th and final tirthankara of the current half-cycle of Jain cosmic time, traditionally regarded as the founder of Jainism as practiced today and dated to the 6th century BCE. Born Vardhamana to King Siddhartha and Queen Trishala, preceded by 14 or 16 auspicious dreams. Renounced society, practiced extreme asceticism including nakedness, and attained Kevala Jnana (omniscience) after years of meditation. Regarded as the successor of the 23rd tirthankara, Parshvanatha, whose teachings he revived and expanded; his oral teachings were compiled by his chief disciple Gautama Swami (Indrabhuti Gautama) into the Agamas.

clues.clue: Identified as the 24th and final Tirthankara, traditionally regarded as the founder of Jainism
examples: name this 24th Tirthankara, a sage often considered the founder of Jainism.
name this figure known by Svetmbaras and Digambaras alike as the twenty fourth tirthankar of Jainism

clues.clue: His mother, Queen Trishala, had 14 or 16 auspicious dreams before his birth, including a pile of jewels, a smokeless fire, a lake of lotuses, a golden vase, and elephant tusks piercing her
examples: A pile of jewels, a smokeless fire, and a lake of lotuses are among the 14 or 16 auspicious dreams that this figure's mother Queen Trishala had before his birth.
his birth was preceded by 16 auspicious dreams including one with 4 elephant tusks piercing his mother.

clues.clue: Attained Kevala Jnana (omniscience) — variously said to be at age 42, 43, or 72 — after up to twelve and a half years of meditation under a Sala or Ashoka tree, in one account slightly before the Buddha's own attainment of nirvana
examples: At age 43, this figure achieved omniscience, or Kevala Jnana, while meditating under a Sala tree.
At the age of 72, this figure attained kevalajnana under the asalah tree as the 24th 'fordmaker.'

clues.clue: Regarded as successor to the 23rd tirthankara Parshvanatha, whose teachings he revived: his mother Trishala was a follower of Parshvanatha, and he added a fifth Great Vow (celibacy) to Parshvanatha's original four, besides urging followers toward complete nakedness
examples: This man succeeded Lord Parshva and while fasting under an ashoka tree, he tore out his hair and took off his clothes before preaching extreme asceticism until his death.
This person's mother Trishala was a notable follower of Lord Parshvanath, but this figure added a fifth Great Vow to Parshvanath's four.

clues.clue: Had a complex relationship with the ascetic Gosala — once a companion, later founder of the rival fatalist Ajivika sect: Gosala tried to thwart one of his prophecies by uprooting a sesame plant, later revived by rain; this figure remained in meditative stillness (kayotsarga) while Gosala misbehaved toward others; the two were once imprisoned as spies at Kuiya until freed by two sisters
examples: Makkhali Gosala uprooted a sesame plant to frustrate a prophecy made by this man that came true when a sudden rain revived it.
This figure would often remain in kayostarga while Gosala would misbehave toward others.

clues.clue: Organized followers into four orders (sadhus, sadhvis, shravakas, shravikas) and had eleven chief disciples (ganadharas) — nine of whom attained kevala jnana in his lifetime; also taught of a life force called jiva
examples: This figure organized his followers into sadhus, sadhvi, shravaka, and shravika, and his teachings were recorded orally by those known as (*) ganadharas.
This figure had eleven disciples, nine of which achieved kevala in his lifetime, and he believed in a life force called jiva.

clues.clue: Said to have been born some 26 or 27 times before his final birth, including as a lion, a demigod (deva), a denizen of hell, and a human
examples: This figure was born as a lion, several times as a demi god, and a human in his twenty-six incarnations prior to to his last birth.
born twenty-seven times, previous spending time as various demigods, Brahmins, and a lion.

clues.clue: Celebrated for achieving moksha/nirvana during Diwali
examples: This figure is celebrated for achieving nirvana during Diwali.
Jains also celebrate Diwali to commemorate the moksha of this 24th and final Tirthankara.

clues.clue: An angry cowherd at Chammani once pierced his ears with thorns/grass sticks; he endured it peacefully
examples: An angry cow herder once stabbed this figure's ears with thorns.
This figure once wandered peacefully after an angry cowherd shoved grass sticks into his ear at Chammani.

clues.clue: His birth (a Kalyanaka) is reenacted in the Snatra puja during a holiday in the month of Chaitra; Indra performed an abhisheka (ceremonial bathing) on him near Vaishali
examples: Events surrounding this event are reenacted in a Snatra puja offered on a holiday during Chaitra celebrating it.
During this event from the most recent occurrence of the five Kalyanakas, which occurred near Vaishali, Indra descended to perform abhisheka on its central subject.

clues.clue: Originally named Vardhamana ("prosperous") for the prosperity his parents' kingdom experienced during the pregnancy; earned the name "Mahavira" ("Great Hero") after remaining unafraid when a heavenly spirit turned into a monster during a childhood game
examples: Due to the prosperity he created in his parent's kingdom while still in the womb, this man was first named Vardaman.
While this figure was playing as a child, a heavenly spirit joined his game and turned into a monster, but this man remained unphased, earning his most common name.

clues.clue: Bitten by the serpent Chandakaushik in a famous episode of suffering
examples: In a famous episode of suffering, this figure would be bitten by the serpent, Chandakaushik.

clues.clue: Once preached for six days straight before disappearing, leaving only his hair and nails behind
examples: In one telling, this figure preached for six days in a row and disappeared leaving his hair and nails.

clues.clue: Also known by the epithet "Sramana" ("ascetic"/"seeker")
examples: 'Sramana' was one of the names given to this religious leader, whose most common name means 'Great Hero.'

clues.clue: His life story is recorded in an easily memorizable form in the Digambara text Tiloya Panatti
examples: Nearly all the events in this person's life are recorded in an easily memorisable form in the Tiloya Panatti.

clues.clue: According to Svetambara tradition, he was first conceived in the womb of Devananda before being transferred to Trishala's
examples: An elephant and a smokeless fire were seen in the first and last of fourteen prophetic dreams which occurred to Trishala before this figure's birth, although he was conceived in Devananda's womb.

clues.clue: Traditionally said to have married Yashoda, with whom he had a daughter
examples: According to some traditions he is married to Yashoda, with whom he had a daughter

clues.clue: Lends his name to the main hall (Mahavira Hall) of Chinese Buddhist temples
examples: This person lends his name to the main hall of Chinese Buddhist temples

clues.clue: In Buddhist texts, carries an epithet meaning "without knot"
examples: his name in Buddhist texts gives him an epithet meaning 'without knot.'

clues.clue: After finding two separate drawings of the Buddha bowing to him, Ashoka ordered the execution of over 18,000 people
examples: After finding two separate drawings of the Buddha bowing to this person, Ashoka ordered the execution of over 18,000 people.

clues.clue: Upali, later a disciple of the Buddha, was originally his follower before losing a debate to his new teacher
examples: Before changing religions, Upali was a disciple of this person before losing a debate to his new teacher.

works.name: Agamas
works.description: The canonical scripture corpus of Jainism, consisting of Mahavira's orally-transmitted teachings, traditionally compiled by his chief disciple Gautama Swami (Indrabhuti Gautama) into twelve texts. Includes the Bhagavati Sutra, which records his dealings with Gosala, and the Kalpa Sutra, which contains biographies of Mahavira and his predecessor Parshvanatha and is recited during Paryushana.

clues.clue: Mahavira's orally-transmitted teachings were compiled by his chief disciple Gautama Swami (Indrabhuti Gautama)
examples: Gautam Swami compiled this figure's teachings into the Agamas.
had their teachings compiled by their chief disciple Indrabhuti Gautama in the Agamas.

clues.clue: The Kalpa Sutra, a variety of Agama, contains biographies of Lords Parshvanatha and Mahavira and is recited (along with Trishala's dreams) during Paryushana; the Tattvartha Sutra holds Jainism's foundational beliefs
examples: The Kalpa one of these texts contains biographies of Lord Parshvanatha and Lord Mahavira, while the Tattvartha one holds the foundational beliefs of Jainism.
an account from the Kalpa Sutra of Trishala Mata's fourteen or sixteen auspicious dreams preceding this event may be recited by Svetambaras.

clues.clue: Comprise twelve texts; include the Bhagavati Sutra, which records Mahavira's teaching of/dealings with Gosala
examples: Name this man whose teaching of Gosala is recorded in the Bhagavati Sutra, one of the twelve Agamas containing the teachings of this Jina and twenty-fourth Tirthankara.

works.name: Ahimsa
works.description: The vow and principle of nonviolence toward all living beings, the foremost of the Five Great Vows and the doctrine most identified with Mahavira's teaching. Upheld through practices like vegetarianism and sweeping insects out of one's path.

clues.clue: Foremost of the Five (Great) Vows; nonviolence extended to all living beings, upheld through vegetarianism and sweeping insects aside with a broom to avoid killing them
examples: Mahavira taught Jains to adhere to this principle, the first of the Five Vows, by doing things like following vegetarian diets. Jains may sweep insects aside with a broom to prevent killing them.
Jains follow a vegetarian diet to fulfill this first of the five vows in Jainism.

clues.clue: Preached/practiced by Mahavira as the supreme moral virtue; traditionally said to have strengthened it in the 6th century BCE, building on Parshvanatha's earlier revival of the idea
examples: Preacher of ahimsa as the supreme moral virtue, name this person often regarded as the founder of Jainism.
Ahimsa was strengthened by this man in the 6th century BCE.

works.name: Aparigraha and Anekantavada
works.description: Two doctrines central to Mahavira's teaching. Aparigraha ("non-possession") is a vow of detachment from people and objects, including sexual relationships and material wealth. Anekantavada ("many-sidedness") holds that truth has many legitimate viewpoints; the later thinker Yashovijaya built the concept of madhyastha on it.

clues.clue: Aparigraha ("non-possession"): a vow of detachment from people and objects, including sexual relationships and material wealth
examples: Another one of Jainism's five vows is this virtue, often translated as 'non-possession,' which Jains adhere to by swearing off attachments like sexual relationships and material wealth.
This successor of Parshvanatha preached aparigraha, or non-possessiveness, and ahimsa, or non-violence.

clues.clue: Anekantavada ("many-sidedness"): the possibility of many legitimate viewpoints; the later Jain thinker Yashovijaya built the concept of madhyastha (praising virtue even outside the faith) on it
examples: This man promoted the philosophy of the possibility of many legitimate viewpoints, Anekantavada.

Yashovijaya developed the concept of madhayastha which allowed for the praising of virtuous qualities even in those not following the faith advocated by this person, building on this person's teaching of anekantavada.

works.name: Paryushana
works.description: A major annual Jain festival (called Das Lakshana by Digambaras) during which the tirthankaras' lives are read from the Kalpa Sutra, including Trishala's dreams before Mahavira's birth. Ends with mutual requests for forgiveness.

clues.clue: Tirthankaras' lives (from the Kalpa Sutra) are read aloud during this festival, including Trishala's 14 or 16 dreams before Mahavira's birth, on the 4th or 5th day
examples: The lives of the tirthankaras, as told in the Kalpa Sutra, are read aloud at this Shvetambara Jain festival.
On the fifth day of Paryushan, Jains commemorate the fourteen dreams Queen Trishala had when she was pregnant with this figure.

clues.clue: Also called Das Lakshana by Digambaras; ends with mutual requests for forgiveness ("Michchhami Dukkadam"), sometimes after a closing day called Samvatsari; can last 8 or 10 days
examples: Name this holy event that Digambaras sometimes call Das Lakshana. On the final day of this event, Jains ask for forgiveness by saying 'Michchhami Dukkadam.'
a monsoon-season festival that ends with Samvatsari, during which people grant 'forgiveness to all living beings' and ask for the same.

clues.clue: Mahavira's birth/birthday is specifically commemorated during this festival
examples: The fourth day of Paryushana coincides with the birthday of this 24th tirthankara, often regarded as the founder of Jainism.
his birth is celebrated during the Paryushan festival.

works.name: Tirthankaras
works.description: The class of 24 tirthankaras ("ford-makers") venerated in Jainism, of whom Mahavira is the 24th and final. Rishabha, the first, is said to have lived roughly 600,000 quintillion years; Parshvanatha, the 23rd, is Mahavira's immediate predecessor. Indra presides over each tirthankara's auspicious life-events (Kalyanakas).

clues.clue: Rishabha (Adinatha, "Lord of Kesariya"), the first tirthankara and son of Marudeva, is said to have lived nearly 600,000 quintillion years and stood over a kilometer tall
examples: The first of these lived for roughly 600,000 quintillion years and is called Rishabha.
Also known as the Lord of Kesariya, this figure was the first thirthankara. This son of Marudeva lived for nearly 600,000 quintillion years and was over a kilometer tall.

clues.clue: Parshvanatha, the 23rd tirthankara and Mahavira's immediate predecessor, taught only four vows (lacking celibacy) before Mahavira added a fifth
examples: Name this twenty-third Tirthankara, whose successor added 'celibacy' to a list of four vows.

clues.clue: The god Indra presides over the auspicious life-events (Kalyanakas) of each tirthankara, appearing as a white elephant in the first prophetic dream announcing a tirthankara's conception
examples: This figure is represented in the first auspicious dream as a white elephant, signifying his role as a guardian deity... in many stories he serves as the teachers of the Tirthankaras.

works.name: Digambara and Svetambara
works.description: The two major Jain sects, both of which revere Mahavira as the 24th tirthankara. Digambara ("sky-clad") ascetics practice complete nakedness, tracing to Mahavira's own teaching; Svetambara ("white-clad") monks wear robes. The sects dispute whether the 19th tirthankara, Mallinatha, was male or female.

clues.clue: Both major sects, Digambara ("sky-clad") and Svetambara ("white-clad"), equally revere Mahavira as the 24th tirthankara
examples: name this figure known by Svetmbaras and Digambaras alike as the twenty fourth tirthankar of Jainism

clues.clue: Mahavira urged followers to eschew clothing entirely; his son-in-law Jamali led the first of seven schisms from his tradition, and sectarian divisions may trace to disputes over whether monks could wear clothes at all
examples: This successor of Parsvanatha urged his followers to eschew clothing. A son-in-law named Jamali led the first of seven schisms from the tradition of this figure.
Sectarian divisions may have arisen because this figure allowed monks to wear clothes.

clues.clue: The sects dispute whether Mallinatha, the 19th tirthankara, was male (Digambara view) or female (Svetambara view), and correspondingly whether women can attain moksha
examples: This 19th tirthankara is depicted as a woman by the Shvetambara sect, despite her very unfair treatment by the Digambara... which teaches that women cannot attain moksha.

comprehensive_summary: Mahavira is the 24th and final tirthankara of Jainism's current cosmic half-cycle, traditionally dated to the 6th century BCE and regarded as the founder of Jainism as practiced today. Born Vardhamana — a name reflecting the prosperity his parents' kingdom experienced during the pregnancy — to King Siddhartha and Queen Trishala, his birth was preceded by 14 or 16 auspicious dreams (a pile of jewels, a smokeless fire, a lake of lotuses, elephant tusks) and, per Svetambara tradition, an embryo-transfer from the womb of Devananda; the event is reenacted in the Snatra puja and included Indra's ceremonial abhisheka near Vaishali. He earned his common name, meaning "Great Hero," after remaining unafraid when a heavenly spirit turned into a monster during a childhood game, and is also known by the epithet "Sramana." Traditionally said to have been born some 26 or 27 times before this final birth — as a lion, a demigod, a denizen of hell, and a human — he renounced his royal life, practicing extreme asceticism including fasting, tearing out his own hair, and going naked, before attaining Kevala Jnana (omniscience) — variously dated to age 42, 43, or 72, after up to twelve and a half years of meditation under a Sala or Ashoka tree. He endured suffering, including a snakebite from the serpent Chandakaushik and having his ears pierced with thorns by an angry cowherd at Chammani, and had a complex relationship with the ascetic Gosala, a former companion who later founded the rival, fatalist Ajivika sect. Mahavira is regarded as the successor of the 23rd tirthankara, Parshvanatha, whose teachings — including four vows — he revived and expanded by adding a fifth Great Vow (celibacy) and by urging complete nakedness, a practice that underlies the later Digambara ("sky-clad") tradition; his son-in-law Jamali led the first of seven schisms from his tradition, and the Digambara-Svetambara split also concerns whether the 19th tirthankara, Mallinatha, was male or female. His central teachings — ahimsa (nonviolence, extended even to insects), aparigraha (non-possession), and anekantavada (the doctrine of many legitimate viewpoints, later developed by Yashovijaya into the concept of madhyastha) — are foundational to Jain practice. He organized followers into four orders (sadhus, sadhvis, shravakas, shravikas) and had eleven chief disciples (ganadharas), nine of whom attained kevala jnana in his lifetime; his oral teachings were compiled by his chief disciple Gautama Swami (Indrabhuti Gautama) into the Agamas, a twelve-text corpus including the Bhagavati Sutra (recording his dealings with Gosala) and the Kalpa Sutra (containing his and Parshvanatha's biographies, read during the festival of Paryushana). Paryushana, also called Das Lakshana by Digambaras, specifically commemorates his birth and ends with mutual requests for forgiveness. He is said to have attained moksha during Diwali, and is traditionally said to have married Yashoda, with whom he had a daughter. Buddhist sources also engage with him: his name lends itself to the main hall of Chinese Buddhist temples, he carries a Buddhist-text epithet meaning "without knot," his follower Upali left him to join the Buddha after losing a debate, and Ashoka reportedly ordered mass executions after finding drawings of the Buddha bowing to him.
recursive_suggestions: Jainism — the religion Mahavira is regarded as founding; already has its own page (output/jainism) with dense cross-linkable content (Tirthankaras, Digambara/Svetambara, Agamas, ahimsa, etc.)
Parshvanatha — Mahavira's predecessor as 23rd tirthankara; only lightly clued here (1 mention) but recurs in Jainism's own page
Gosala / Ajivika — Mahavira's former companion and rival sect founder; flag as a possible Philosophy/Religion crossover if a set clues the Ajivikas heavily
Paryushana — could deserve its own deep-dive page if future sets clue it beyond what's captured here; watch for overlap with this page's Holiday section

Note: 'Nalanda mahavira' (a 2023 tossup, answer 'Nalanda mahavira') was discarded as off-topic — it concerns the Buddhist monastic university at Nalanda (Bihar), not this figure; a coincidental answerline collision, not a genuine Mahavira clue
